ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese spicy rice salad. FOODON_00002809 (rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Burmese rice salad. A000E (rice-based mixed dish) is used as the closest parent. Egg component noted as secondary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Burmese spicy rice salad. No suitable parent code identified in SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

preparation_method: mixed with chilli oil and aromatics
main_ingredients: fish sauce (may be omitted for vegetarian)
allergens: fish (fish sauce, dried shrimp — may be present)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Burmese spicy rice salad with fried egg is approximately 300 g, comprising roughly 200 g cooked rice, one fried egg (~60 g), and approximately 40 g of seasoning, aromatics, and chilli dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Cooked white rice and egg-based mixed rice dish — estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (NDB 20444) and fried egg (NDB 01129), combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA condiment data for chilli-based dressings
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Burmese spicy rice salad exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy cooked white rice (~67% by weight), fried egg (~20% by weight), and chilli-sesame-lime dressing (~13% by weight). Sodium elevated to reflect fish sauce and seasoning typical of this dish. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled, Asian-style) GI ~64
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese spicy rice salad. GI of 64 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice (Asian-style, medium-grain), which constitutes approximately 67% of the dish by weight. The addition of egg, chilli, lime juice, and sesame oil may modestly lower the effective GI; however, the white rice GI of 64 is retained as a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 67.5) / 100 = 43.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A standard 300 g serving provides approximately 960 mg sodium, which represents 42–48% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index from white rice base (GI ~64, medium)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice base contributes a medium GI of approximately 64 and a glycemic load of ~43 per serving, which may cause a significant postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider smaller portions, substituting brown rice, or pairing with additional protein and fibre to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains approximately 3 g saturated fat and 960 mg sodium per serving; while saturated fat is within acceptable limits, the sodium load warrants attention for individuals with cardiovascular disease, and preparation with reduced fish sauce and sesame oil is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Rice Salad with Fried Egg (No Meat) is a Burmese dish known in Burmese as ဇလုံ ထမင်းနယ် ကြက်ဥကြော်ပါမယ် (Za-lone Htamin Hnyet Kyat-u-kyaw Pa-meh). It consists of cooked white rice tossed with a spicy, tangy dressing made from chilli oil, fish sauce, lime juice, shallots, garlic, and sesame oil, topped with a fried egg. The 'no meat' variant omits chicken or pork that would otherwise be included, making it suitable for those avoiding meat while retaining egg. The dish has a bold, complex flavour profile — spicy from fresh and dried chilli, sour from lime, savoury from fish sauce, and nutty from sesame. The texture contrasts soft, slightly sticky rice with the crispy edges of the fried egg. It is a common street food and casual restaurant dish in Myanmar and is served at Burmese restaurants in Singapore such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal (~148 kcal per 100 g) with meaningful protein from the egg (~5.2 g per 100 g). The white rice base gives a medium glycemic index of approximately 64, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium is elevated (~320 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce and seasoning, which is a consideration for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish provides small amounts of iron, calcium, B vitamins, and vitamin A from the egg and aromatics. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Burmese spicy rice salad exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice and fried egg, with sodium adjusted upward to reflect fish sauce seasoning typical of this dish. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice (GI 64). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy components (cooked white rice NDB 20444, fried egg NDB 01129) and FAO/INFOODS SEA condiment data; no direct approved-source entry exists for this dish. 2. Sodium of 320 mg per 100 g is an upward adjustment from plain rice to reflect fish sauce and seasoning; actual sodium will vary by restaurant preparation. 3. GI of 64 is analogous from University of Sydney GI Database boiled white rice; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this estimation. 5. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the composite source data used for this estimation. 6. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the rice and egg components; no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation. 7. The 'no meat' designation is taken from the field survey listing; the dish retains egg and may retain fish sauce and dried shrimp unless specifically requested otherwise. 8. Dietary flag is_vegetarian is set to false because fish sauce and dried shrimp are typical ingredients; the dish is not reliably vegetarian unless specifically prepared without these. 9. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided to satisfy Burmese cuisine served in Singapore context under the Veritas multilingual rule for Singaporean-served dishes. 10. GL per serving = (64 × 22.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.0 serving size factor) / 100 = (64 × 67.5) / 100 = 43.2.
